data.title: OFS Credit lowers estimate for NAV per share in March
data.description:
OFS Credit Company (NASDAQ:OCCI) said Tuesday it lowered its estimate for net asset value per share to $6.57-$6.67 as of March 31, 2025, from the prior estimate of $6.75-$6.85 at Feb. 28, 2025. 

The preliminary March estimate did not undergo the company’s typical quarter-end financial closing procedures.

OCCI shares gained 1.2% in after-hours trading.

data.title: Lithia & Driveway announces COO transition
data.description:
• Lithia & Driveway (NYSE:LAD) has announced that Adam Chamberlain will transition from his current role as Executive Vice President and COO effective June 1, 2025, to join Mercedes-Benz as CEO of Mercedes-Benz USA.
  • "His contributions have helped strengthen our operations, sharpen our offerings and customer awareness, drive growth, and advance our strategy. This is the beginning of a new chapter in our long-standing partnership with Mercedes-Benz, and Adam remains committed to our shared success." said Bryan DeBoer, President and CEO of Lithia & Driveway.

data.title: Paul Mueller Company further expands components products facility
data.description:
• Paul Mueller Company (OTCPK:MUEL) on Tuesday announced a $17.9M expansion to its components products facility which will increase the capacity for producing tank heads, cones, and shells.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Berkshire's HomeServices of America names new CEO
data.description:
HomeServices of America, a subsidiary of Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.B) (NYSE:BRK.A), named Chris Kelly to lead the real estate company as its president and CEO, succeeding Gino Blefari, who becomes chairman emeritus, effective immediately, the company said on Tuesday.

Kelly brings more than 25 years of experience to the role. He joined HomeServices in 2007 as general counsel and most recently served as the company's executive vice president.

Blefari will continue as a strategic adviser to the organization, including to the Berkshire Hathaway HomeServices franchise organization as it begins the process of expanding its executive team.

The company's businesses include brokerage, mortgage, franchising, title, escrow, insurance and relocation services. HomeServices of America is the owner of the Berkshire Hathaway HomeServices franchise network.

data.title: Omnicom cites tough macro backdrop for its beat-and-miss Q1
data.description:
U.S. advertising and marketing company Omnicom (NYSE:OMC) delivered a mixed earnings report for the first quarter of 2025, with a top-line miss and a bottom-line beat, as it navigated a "challenging economic environment."

Shares of the company were down 4.3% during after-hours on Tuesday but pared some losses.

The impact of foreign currency translation reduced revenue by $59.2M for the company in Q1. It also saw organic growth declines of 3.6% for the Other North America segment, 0.7% in the UK, and 9.3% in the Middle East & Africa.

For the total company, organic revenue growth for Q1 was 3.4%.

"We are assessing the implications of economic and market events to determine how they will affect our clients and business for the remainder of 2025," CEO John Wren said.

Wren also expects to close the Interpublic (NYSE:IPG) deal in the second half of 2025 and said the combined company will have "substantial opportunities for revenue growth and distinctive cost synergy potential to drive increased profitability, EPS growth, and free cash flow."

Net income to Omnicom for the three months ended March 31 was $287.7M, or $1.45 per share, compared with $318.6M, or $1.59 per share, for the same period last year. The fall in net income was driven by a $122.2M surge in total expenses.

On an adjusted basis, the company earned $1.70 per share, ahead of the average analyst estimate by 4 cents.

Revenue rose 1.7% to $3.69B, but missed estimates by $30M.

data.title: Earnings Snapshot: Omnicom misses on topline, net income falls 9.7% in Q1
Ostin Technology announces pricing of $5M registered direct offering
data.description:
• Ostin Technology (NASDAQ:OST) said on Tuesday that it has entered into a securities purchase agreement with several investors for the purchase and sale of (i) 9.09M Class A ordinary shares and (ii) Class A ordinary share purchase warrants to purchase up to 90.9M Class A  ordinary shares.
  • Each Class A ordinary share is being sold together with two associated warrants, each to purchase one Class A ordinary share at a combined offering price of $0.55 per share and associated warrants.
  • The warrants have an exercise price of $0.8 per Class A ordinary share, which will be immediately exercisable after issuance, and will expire two years from the date of issuance. 
  • Aggregate gross proceeds are expected to be approximately $5M. 
  • Ostin Technology (NASDAQ:OST) shares were down about 7% in extended trading.

data.title: Boeing's stock slides on reports of Chinese limits on U.S. aviation giant
data.description:
Boeing’s (NYSE:BA) shares on Tuesday fell 2.4% following multiple news reports that Chinese regulators told domestic airlines to halt new orders of planes from the U.S. aviation giant and to obtain official clearance before accepting delivery of previously purchased jets.

The move is another hurdle for Boeing (NYSE:BA) as it navigates escalating trade friction and economic headwinds. The company has long benefited from tariff-free access to key global markets, but is now facing mounting pressure from retaliatory trade measures.

The end of its decades-long duty-free treatment, coupled with broader global economic uncertainty, is creating serious challenges for the company’s extensive and vulnerable supply chain. U.S. tariffs and retaliatory measures by other countries are also dampening demand for air travel, compounding Boeing’s (BA) difficulties.

The company, which reported $67 billion in revenue last year, is already under financial strain. After burning through $14 billion in 2024, Boeing (BA) had aimed to return to positive cash flow by the end of this year, a goal now clouded by geopolitical and operational uncertainty.

Airbus rivalry

Analysts have warned that the evolving trade environment may benefit Boeing’s (BA) chief rival, Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) (OTCPK:EADSY), which already has a stronger foothold in China and operates final-assembly lines there.

While some existing deliveries to China could still proceed, airlines may choose to delay accepting aircraft to avoid high import tariffs. Boeing (BA) could take a financial hit of approximately $1.2 billion this year if all deliveries to China are halted, according to estimates from equity research firm Bernstein.

So far in 2025, Boeing (BA) has delivered 130 jets worldwide, 18 of which went to Chinese carriers.

Jetliners are among the most expensive items airlines purchase, with the 737 Max carrying a list price of more than $100 million. In recent years, demand for new aircraft soared as the aviation industry rebounded from the pandemic, but that momentum is showing signs of slowing. Rising travel costs and concerns over the economy are prompting some U.S. travelers to cancel or delay plans.

Ryanair (RYAAY) Chief Executive Michael O’Leary, in a recent interview with the Financial Times, said the airline might postpone receipt of 25 Boeing (BA) jets originally scheduled for August, potentially pushing deliveries into next year.

Supply-chain worries

Boeing (BA) Chief Executive Kelly Ortberg has expressed concern about the broader implications of tariffs, not just the direct cost of duties on aircraft parts and materials, but also the impact on global export capabilities and the overall resilience of the company’s supply network.

Although Boeing's (BA) current backlog exceeds 5,500 aircraft — enough to keep production lines running for years — the longer-term implications of restricted access to China, which is projected to represent 20% of global aircraft demand, could be severe.

China's domestically produced Comac C919 is not yet a full competitor. The company delivered just 13 jets in 2024 and still relies heavily on U.S.-made components. However, it may gain market share if Chinese carriers shift further away from Boeing (BA) amid trade tensions.

Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) (OTCPK:EADSY) has outpaced Boeing (BA) in China for several years and may also benefit if tariffs continue to make U.S.-built aircraft less economically viable. Unlike Boeing (BA), which only operates a completion facility in China, Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) (OTCPK:EADSY) assembles aircraft in the country, giving it a logistical and political advantage.

Boeing’s (BA) supplier base, already battered by pandemic-era disruptions and last year’s widely reported manufacturing flaws — including a mid-flight panel failure on an Alaska Airlines jet — could face additional strain.

Many suppliers are smaller, financially fragile firms contending with labor shortages and rising input costs. The breakdown of a long-standing trade agreement that once shielded aircraft manufacturing from global tariffs is now threatening to further destabilize the sector.

data.title: Equity Bancshares GAAP EPS of $0.85 beats by $0.02, revenue of $60.62M beats by $3.93M
data.description:
• Equity Bancshares press release (NYSE:EQBK): Q1 GAAP EPS of $0.85 beats by $0.02.
  • Revenue of $60.62M (+8.4% Y/Y) beats by $3.93M.
